title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Sharp Decline in CA LCFS Trade Volumes and Prices; Type 1 Trades Drop 73.65% WoW, Prices Fall 7.34%
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights: (2nd June â€“ 8th June)
  • A total of 192,151 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 73.65% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 33.30% WoW as 1,484,343 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $47.36/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 7.34% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 0.63% in price, reaching $55.27/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 6.31% WoW; on 10th June, the spot price stood at $40.25/MT, and as of 9th June, the benchmark price stood at $46.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 2.58% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 9.18% WoW.
cCarbonâ€™s Market View Type 1 Trade Prices and spot prices declined last week, continuing their downward trend since CARB resubmitted the LCFS amendments to the OAL. Last week, California Senate Minority Leader Brian Jones pushed for a vote on SB 2, a policy directed at repealing the LCFS updates adopted by CARB. Senate Democrats rejected the measure, keeping the current Low Carbon Fuel Standard in place. Meanwhile, we await an update on the progress of LCFS amendments submitted by CARB to the Office of Administrative Law, which would decide on the amendments by the end of June.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 7.34% WoW fall, decreasing from $51.11 to $47.36 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 0.62% WoW, from $55.62 to $55.27. Type 1 trades saw 192,151 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 73.65% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 33.30%, reaching 1,484,343 credits. [caption id="attachment_598304" align="alignnone" width="1001"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 6.31% WoW, reaching $44.65/MT. On 10th June, the spot price was $40.25/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $46.00/MT as of 9th June. Â  [caption id="attachment_598305" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 60 trades were reported in the market, with 12 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.25 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 70,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $43.00 per credit of Type 1 reported for one transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 300,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $52.79 and $56.75, and the smallest volume transfers of 2 credits were undertaken for a price of $50.00. [caption id="attachment_598306" align="alignnone" width="1003"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and

The average spot price in June for LCFS Type 1 credits stood at $47.36 through the week of June 2nd, and the total number of trading volumes transacted for the same period stood at 1.48 MMT.   [caption id="attachment_598307" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 2nd June â€“ 8th June, total VPT recorded a 2.84% decline WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices. [caption id="attachment_598308" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 2.58% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 9.18% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • White House Considers Plan to Clear Record Backlog of Small Refinery Biofuel Waivers: The White House is weighing a plan to clear a record backlog of requests from small refineries for exemptions from U.S. biofuel laws, which could include approving many current applications and requesting industry input to deal with older ones, according to three sources familiar with the plans.
  • DOE Updates 45ZCF-GREET Model: The U.S. Department of Energyâ€™s Bioenergy Technologies Office (BETO) on May 30 released an updated version of its 45ZCF-GREET modeling tool to account for new feedstocks and methods of production, including ethanol from corn wet-milling and natural gas from coal-mine methane.
  • Washington State Opens 2025 Clean Fuel Standard Credit Clearance Market: Washingtonâ€™s Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) program aims to reduce transportation-related greenhouse gas emissions by 45% from 2017 levels by 2038. Covering both 2023 and 2024, the first compliance period saw 200 entities reporting fuel data, generating 1.96 million deficits and 4.83 million credits. Of the 61 entities with deficits, 57 met compliance through credit purchases or retirements.
  • Senate Rejects Effort to Roll Back LCFS Gas Price Increase and Fuel Regulations: Senate Minority Leader Brian Jones (R-San Diego) forced a floor vote on Senate Bill 2, his legislation to repeal Governor Gavin Newsomâ€™s 65-cent gas price hike. Senate Democrats unanimously voted to block the bill. SB 2 would have immediately halted the costly update to the stateâ€™s Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS).
  • Record Number of Renewable Fuel Infrastructure Grants Awarded in Iowa: During its last scheduled meeting during Fiscal Year 2025, the Iowa Renewable Fuels Infrastructure Program (RFIP) today awarded more than $1 million in cost-share grants to support the installation of E15 at 91 additional retail fuel sites across Iowa. This milestone brings the total number of E15 projects awarded this fiscal year to 388, by far the most grants in a year since RFIPâ€™s inception.
